coredata.dc:title: Current methods for peroxide detection in food and the emerging role of heteroaromatic dyes as an efficient alternative
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

Nordin, Nurdiana

Abdullah, Zanariah
coredata.dc:description: The assessment of peroxides in food products plays a critical role in determining their oxidative deterioration and overall quality. Conventional methods for peroxide detection often involve intricate and time-consuming procedures, prompting a growing interest in developing efficient and rapid techniques for detecting peroxides in food. An emerging approach in this endeavor involves the use of heteroaromatic dyes within peroxide detection systems. Heteroaromatic dyes exhibit inherent reactivity towards peroxides, resulting in noticeable color changes or fluorescence signals, making them widely applicable in biosensors. This review article explores recent advancements in harnessing heteroaromatic dyes for biosensors tailored to peroxide detection in food. It examines the fundamental principles governing the interaction between heteroaromatic dyes and peroxides, emphasizing factors that influence sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, the review critically evaluates the advantages and limitations of utilizing these dyes for peroxide detection. Lastly, the article contemplates future prospects and potential research directions within this evolving domain.

head.ce:title: Current methods for peroxide detection in food and the emerging role of heteroaromatic dyes as an efficient alternative

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The assessment of peroxides in food products plays a critical role in determining their oxidative deterioration and overall quality. Conventional methods for peroxide detection often involve intricate and time-consuming procedures, prompting a growing interest in developing efficient and rapid techniques for detecting peroxides in food. An emerging approach in this endeavor involves the use of heteroaromatic dyes within peroxide detection systems. Heteroaromatic dyes exhibit inherent reactivity towards peroxides, resulting in noticeable color changes or fluorescence signals, making them widely applicable in biosensors. This review article explores recent advancements in harnessing heteroaromatic dyes for biosensors tailored to peroxide detection in food. It examines the fundamental principles governing the interaction between heteroaromatic dyes and peroxides, emphasizing factors that influence sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, the review critically evaluates the advantages and limitations of utilizing these dyes for peroxide detection. Lastly, the article contemplates future prospects and potential research directions within this evolving domain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring the safety and quality of food products is a critical concern in the realm of consumer health and satisfaction. One pivotal aspect of this endeavor is the timely detection of peroxides, which serve as vital indicators of oxidative deterioration within various food items. Hydroperoxides, in particular, offer insights into the extent of spoilage, stability, and overall freshness of edibles (Fu et al., 2020; Jadhav et al., 1995; Shahidi and John, 2013). However, conventional methods employed for peroxide detection often involve intricate and time-consuming procedures (Abeyrathne et al., 2021), prompting the exploration of more expedient and effective approaches.

Amidst the pursuit of enhanced detection techniques, heteroaromatic dyes have emerged as promising candidates for rapid peroxide detection. These dyes possess innate reactivity towards peroxides, resulting in distinctive alterations in color or the emission of fluorescence upon interaction (Kaur and Choi, 2015; Lee et al., 2012; Rezende et al., 2018). This property makes them suitable candidates for developing sensitive and selective detection platforms. The interaction between heteroaromatic dye and peroxides is based on their chemical properties. Peroxides, including hydroperoxides and peroxyl radicals, can undergo oxidative reactions with heteroaromatic dyes, resulting in structural changes that manifest as distinct color or fluorescence alterations (Er et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2012; Traven and Cheptsov, 2020). These changes can be quantitatively correlated with the peroxide concentration, enabling reliable determination of peroxides in food samples. Beyond their reactivity, heteroaromatic dyes have expanded their applications into domains such as biosensors, presenting an intriguing avenue for analytical innovation. Their ability to promptly respond to the presence of peroxides offers the potential to revolutionize conventional detection methods, enabling more efficient and real-time assessments.
This review provides insights into peroxide level determination and elucidates the interplay between heteroaromatic dyes and peroxides for rapid peroxide detection in food.

The assessment of peroxide levels plays a pivotal role in evaluating the quality and oxidative state of food items, particularly those containing lipids. Over time, a spectrum of analytical methods has emerged to quantify peroxides in diverse food samples, each presenting its unique strengths and complexities. These methods can be broadly categorized into direct and straightforward techniques, such as UV spectrophotometer assays, colorimetric iodine, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and electrochemical methods. Alternatively, there are more intricate approaches, including iodometric titration methods, chromatographic techniques, fluorescence assays, and NMR assays. It is crucial to acknowledge that these methods have evolved independently without following a specific chronological timeline. In this classification, we outline the distinctive attributes of each method, providing a comprehensive view of their utilization and intricacy in determining peroxide values within food products.

Spectrophotometric methods are widely used for peroxide value (PV) determination due to their simplicity and ease of operation. These assays rely on the reaction between peroxides and specific reagents, producing colored compounds whose intensity is directly proportional to the peroxide concentration. For instance, the UV method relies on measuring the absorbance of peroxides at specific wavelengths in the ultraviolet region. UV spectroscopy offers advantages such as simplicity and rapid analysis. However, it does have limitations in terms of sensitivity and specificity. Some food components and impurities may exhibit similar absorbance profiles, leading to potential interferences and inaccurate results.

For example, Fe(III)-thiocyanate methods are widely employed for determining the peroxide value in lipid oxidation analysis. These methods rely on the redox potential of ferric ions, which act as catalysts in lipid oxidation reactions(Feng et al., 2020). The Fe(III)-thiocyanate method offers simplicity and sensitivity, based on the oxidation of Fe(II) to Fe(III) by peroxides in an acidic environment, followed by a reaction with thiocyanate. The resulting Fe(III)-thiocyanate complex exhibits strong absorption at 500–510 nm (Murecsan et al., 2010). However, the accuracy of this method can be influenced by the presence of carotenoids in oils. To overcome this limitation, a modification involving the extraction of pigments using an organic solvent prior to peroxide value determination has been introduced, resulting in improved sensitivity with minimal sample requirements (Hornero-Méndez et al., 2001).

The principle of the Colorimetric Iodine Method is based on the reaction between iodine and peroxides in an acidic medium. In this method, a sample is mixed with an acidic solution containing potassium iodide (KI). The peroxides present in the sample oxidize the iodide ions (I−) to form iodine (I2). The generated iodine then reacts with excess potassium iodide to form triiodide ions (I3 −), resulting in the release of iodine from the solution. The concentration of released iodine is directly proportional to the amount of peroxides in the sample.

To quantify the peroxide concentration, spectrophotometric measurements are taken of the color change caused by the liberated iodine over the wavelength range of 210–350 nm. The solution transitions from colorless to a characteristic blue or brown hue, the intensity of which is directly related to the concentration of peroxides in the sample.

The Colorimetric Iodine Method offers several advantages, including simplicity, rapidity, and relatively low cost. It enables both visual and quantitative assessments of peroxide levels in a sample. Nonetheless, it exhibits limitations, notably susceptibility to interference from other compounds and potential inaccuracies when the sample contains substances that react with iodine. Susceptibility to Interference from Other Compounds: One of the primary limitations of this method is its vulnerability to interference from other compounds present in the sample. Compounds sharing similar chemical properties or reactivity with iodine can disrupt the reaction between iodine and peroxides, resulting in inaccuracies in peroxide level determination. These interferences can lead to false readings, undermining the measurement's reliability. Potential Inaccuracies with Reactive Substances: Samples containing substances that react with iodine, such as certain antioxidants or reducing agents, can cause potential inaccuracies in determining peroxide levels. These reactive substances may alter the reaction dynamics by consuming or interacting with iodine, thereby affecting the color change and subsequently impacting the accuracy of peroxide concentration measurement.

In some instances, peroxide levels can also be determined at 358 or 400 nm using the colorimetric iodine method variant, offering quicker reaction times and lower sample requirements (Takagi et al., 1978). However, these old conventional spectrophotometric techniques are relatively intricate due to multiple reagent-dependent steps. In response, an effective UV spectrometric approach for determining peroxide value has emerged. Gotoh et al. introduced a straightforward method to quantify the PV in colored lipids (Gotoh et al., 2011). The method relied on the reaction between triphenylphosphine (TPP) and oxidized oil, producing triphenylphosphineoxide (TPPO). Diphenylphosphineoxide (DPPO) acted as an internal standard. TPPO generated from the reaction was analyzed using high-pressure liquid chromatography–UV spectroscopy, detecting absorption at 260 nm. They identified optimal conditions for the most accurate calibration curve, establishing a 2:1 TPP–oxidized oil mix ratio, a reaction temperature of 40°C, and a reaction time of 30 minutes. The method's quantification limit was determined to be 2.01 per mol hydroperoxyl group, equivalent to a PV value of 0.2 meq/kg in a 10-mg oil sample (Gotoh et al., 2011). These TPPO-based methodologies exhibit simplicity, speed, and potential as alternatives to traditional approaches, providing quantitative determination of peroxide value. However, they share concerns regarding susceptibility to interference and potential inaccuracies similar to those of the Colorimetric Iodine Method.

FTIR spectroscopy plays a crucial role in peroxide detection by providing insights into the molecular changes induced by peroxide presence in various samples, including food products. It harnesses the interaction between infrared light and molecular vibrations, offering a powerful tool to analyze the oxidative state of compounds.

To employ FTIR spectroscopy for peroxide detection, a sample is exposed to infrared radiation, and the resulting spectrum is obtained by measuring the wavelengths of light absorbed by the sample. Researchers compare the obtained spectrum to reference spectra of known compounds or analyze changes in specific absorption bands to infer the presence and extent of peroxides in the sample.

FTIR spectroscopy offers several advantages in peroxide detection. It provides a non-destructive analysis method, preserving the sample's integrity (Yi et al., 2019). Additionally, it allows rapid and simultaneous analysis of multiple samples, suitable for high-throughput applications (Jamwal et al., 2021). FTIR spectroscopy identifies various forms of peroxides, including hydroperoxides, alkoxyl radicals, peroxyl radicals, and secondary oxidation products such as aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, and other volatile compounds. These compounds contribute to off-flavors and deterioration of the oil, thereby offering a comprehensive understanding of oxidative changes in the sample (Cebi et al., 2017). However, FTIR spectroscopy in peroxide detection also has limitations. Sample preparation is critical, as factors such as sample thickness and homogeneity can influence the results. Expertise in analyzing complex vibrational modes and understanding specific chemical changes associated with peroxides is essential for interpreting FTIR spectra.

Talpur et al. utilized single bounce attenuated total reflectance (SB-ATR) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy along with chemometrics to precisely determine multiple parameters, including the PV in cottonseed oil during the frying of French frozen potato chips at 170°C (Talpur et al., 2014). They developed robust calibrations using techniques such as Partial Least Square (PLS), Stepwise Multiple Linear Regression (SMLR), Principal Component Regression (PCR), and Simple Beer's Law (SBL), resulting in excellent regression coefficients (R² > 0.992) for all parameters. Additionally, the root mean square error of prediction (RMSEP) was below 1.95% for all determinations. Their study concluded that SB-ATR FTIR, combined with multivariate chemometrics, provided an accurate and simultaneous means of determining various parameters during the frying process, eliminating the necessity for toxic organic solvents. However, while demonstrating efficacy, this method might encounter challenges in handling heterogeneous or complex sample matrices, which could potentially impact measurement accuracy.

Electrochemical methods have gained significant attention in the detection of peroxides, especially hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), due to their sensitivity, selectivity, and potential for real-time monitoring (Gülcin, 2012; Harfield et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2022). These methods can be broadly categorized into enzymatic and nonenzymatic approaches, each with its own advantages and challenges.

Enzymatic electrochemical sensors utilize enzymes such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP) or glucose oxidase (GOx) to catalyze the reduction of H2O2 (Nordin et al., 2021; Ramanavicius et al., 2015). The generated current or potential change is proportional to the H2O2 concentration. These sensors offer excellent selectivity and sensitivity due to the enzymes' specificity for H2O2 (Nordin et al., 2021). However, immobilizing enzymes onto electrode surfaces can be complex and time-consuming. Additionally, enzyme activity is sensitive to environmental conditions such as temperature, pH, and humidity.

On the other hand, nonenzymatic electrochemical sensors directly detect H2O2 without relying on enzymes, offering simplicity and stability compared to enzymatic sensors. Common electrode materials for nonenzymatic H2O2 sensing include noble metals (e.g., platinum, gold), metal oxides (e.g., manganese oxide, nickel oxide), and carbon-based materials (e.g., graphene, carbon nanotubes) (Dong et al., 2021; W. Liu et al., 2021; Song et al., 2010). These materials catalyze the electrochemical reduction of peroxide, resulting in a measurable current or potential change (Dong et al., 2021; W. Liu et al., 2021; Sakthivel et al., 2019). A third approach known as hybrid and nanostructured electrode materials combines the advantages of enzymatic and nonenzymatic approaches 2014; Tee et al., 2017). These materials can enhance catalytic activity and stability. For example, nanostructured gold or platinum catalysts can be combined with enzymes or polymers to improve sensor performance (Bi et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2014).

Iodometric titration is a well-established method commonly employed for determining peroxide value. This approach involves the reaction of peroxides in the food sample with iodide ions, resulting in the formation of iodine. The liberated iodine is then quantified by titration with a standardized solution of thiosulfate (Na2S2O3) (AOCS, 1989; AOCS, 1998). For instance, iodometric titration is widely used for the quantitative determination of H2O2 concentration (Klassen et al., 1994). This method relies on the redox reaction between H2O2 and iodide ions (I−) in an acidic solution. The reaction produces iodine (I2) and water, and the formed iodine is subsequently titrated with a standardized Na2S2O3 solution. The endpoint of the titration is detected using a starch indicator, forming a deep blue complex with iodine.

The balanced chemical equation for the iodometric titration of H2O2 can be represented as:
H2O2 + 2H+ + 2I− → I2 + 2H2O

The liberated iodine reacts with thiosulfate ions in the titrant:
I2 + 2S2O3 2− → 2I− + S4O6 2−
The starch indicator is added toward the end of the titration, and the appearance of a persistent blue color indicates the completion of the reaction.

While iodometric titration is straightforward and relatively inexpensive, it does have limitations in terms of sensitivity and selectivity. Interference from other substances present in the food matrix, such as reducing agents or transition metals, can affect the accuracy and reliability of the results (AOCS, 1989; Choe and Min, 2006; Cirlini et al., 2012). Moreover, iodometric titration may not be as sensitive as modern instrumental techniques like electrochemical or spectrophotometric methods.

Chromatographic techniques have found extensive applications in detecting and quantifying peroxides due to their high sensitivity, selectivity, and ability to separate and analyze complex mixtures. Two main chromatographic techniques used for peroxide detection are High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) and Gas Chromatography (GC), both of which can provide accurate and reliable results.

HPLC and GC are indirect methods that offer higher specificity and sensitivity for peroxide determination. These chromatographic techniques separate peroxides or their decomposition products from the sample matrix and detect them using specific detectors. HPLC is a versatile technique employing a liquid mobile phase for separations. In peroxide detection, HPLC can be coupled with various detectors, such as UV–visible, fluorescence, or electrochemical detectors, to quantify peroxide. The common approach involves the reaction of peroxide with a specific reagent, producing a detectable compound separated and quantified using HPLC. For example, the Horiba D-xylose method couples HPLC with a fluorescence detector to detect peroxide (Park et al., 2023; Shearer et al., 2023). This method involves the reaction of peroxide with D-xylose and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) to generate fluorescence-active products, which are separated and quantified using HPLC with fluorescence detection. On the other hand, GC is commonly used to analyze volatile and semi-volatile compounds. In peroxide detection, GC can separate and quantify volatile products resulting from the reaction of peroxide with specific reagents. This is particularly useful when analyzing gaseous samples containing H2O2 (Fei et al., 2023; Sefati et al., 2023). For instance, GC-based peroxide detection involves the reaction of peroxide with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) to form dinitrophenylhydrazones (Sousa et al., 2017; Vento et al., 2014). These derivatives are volatile and can be separated by GC, and their quantification is achieved by comparing the peak area or height with a standard curve.

Fluorescence-based methods utilize specific fluorescent probes or indicators designed for peroxides. The emission of fluorescent light is directly proportional to the peroxide concentration, enabling sensitive and selective detection. Fluorescence assays offer exceptional sensitivity and are adept at detecting low peroxide levels (Dickinson and Chang, 2008; Gülcin, 2012). Two commonly used fluorescence-based assay techniques for peroxide detection are fluorescence spectroscopy and fluorescence microscopy. Fluorescence spectroscopy involves measuring the intensity and wavelength of emitted fluorescence from a sample. In peroxide detection, fluorescent probes reacting with H2O2 are utilized (Gao et al., 2023). These probes undergo specific reactions with H2O2, leading to changes in their fluorescence properties, such as intensity or wavelength. One common mechanism used in designing fluorescent probes for H2O2 involves boronates or boronic acids as recognition moieties. These boronic acid-based probes can react with H2O2, oxidizing the boronic acid to form a phenol group. Additionally, the boronic acid moiety in the probe interacts with H2O2, forming a cyclic boronate ester intermediate (Gao et al., 2023). Monitoring these changes allows quantification of the peroxide concentration.

Another example is 2’,7’-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), a non-fluorescent probe, is cleaved by cellular esterases, resulting in a fluorescent compound (Wang et al., 2005). In the presence of peroxide, DCFH-DA is oxidized to form the highly fluorescent 2’,7’-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) (Rastogi et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2005). The increase in fluorescence intensity is proportional to the concentration of peroxide. The second technique based on fluorescence assays is florescence microscopy. This technique enables the visualization of fluorescent signals at the cellular or subcellular level. Various fluorescent probes, such as Amplex® UltraRed, react with peroxide in the presence of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), producing a fluorescent product (Gantumur et al., 2019; Kamada et al., 2011). Techniques like confocal microscopy in fluorescence microscopy allow visualization of peroxide distribution within living cells and tissues. This offers valuable insights into the spatial and temporal dynamics of peroxide generation and its role in cellular processes (Kamada et al., 2011).

In recent years, 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy has emerged as a prominent analytical technique for determining the peroxide value in edible oils. Exploiting the fact that the integration of 1H NMR signals is directly proportional to the abundance of protons in each nucleus, the molality of peroxide moieties can be derived from NMR spectra with remarkable precision (Beyer et al., 2010; Salinero et al., 2012). This approach is distinguished by its elegant simplicity, time-efficient nature, and capacity to unravel intricate aspects of the sample through a single NMR measurement.

The 1H NMR technology was utilized to ascertain the peroxide value of fish oils by examining the ratio of olefinic and aliphatic protons within fatty acids (Shi et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2017). Although this method employed minimal sample volume, its sensitivity was found to be modest. This method harnesses the power of the Deming regression equation established a robust correlation between peroxide values obtained through iodometry and those derived from 1H NMR analysis, yielding highly satisfactory outcomes (Merkx et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2019). Additionally, Hafer et al. explored the potential of the TPP-TPPO reaction-based strategy, showing promise for determining peroxide values at lower levels (<10 meq/kg) (Hafer et al., 2020). While NMR spectroscopy stands as an invaluable tool for the quantitative assessment of peroxide value in oils, its practical utility remains contingent upon the availability of specialized instrumentation within laboratory settings.

In the realm of conventional analytical methods such as UV spectroscopy, fluorescence assays, and colorimetric assays employed for peroxide detection, ongoing advancements drive researchers to explore innovative avenues enhancing sensitivity, selectivity, and applicability. A particularly promising direction involves the advent of heteroaromatic dye precursors designed for selective action upon interaction with hydrogen peroxide. This activation, whether manifested as fluorescence emission, UV spectroscopic absorption, or discernible color changes visible to the naked eye, holds immense potential. The subsequent section will provide a concise overview of the emergence, development, and utilization of heteroaromatic dyes in the context of biosensors designed for peroxide detection.

Most of the known methods for determining peroxide value in edible oils have been extensively utilized globally, with each method demonstrating its optimal capabilities. However, even with the best results achievable, these methods have inherent limitations. In this review, a comprehensive discussion regarding the advantages and limitations of each method is presented (refer to Table 1). Despite the distinct advantages and drawbacks of these techniques, they remain essential tools for peroxide detection. It is crucial to continually optimize and refine these methods to mitigate their limitations. Additionally, exploring alternative methods is imperative to find more effective approaches for determining peroxide value. Researchers should strive to innovate and address the challenges posed by these various methods. Overcoming these challenges will expand the applications of peroxide detection techniques in the realm of edible oils. Hence, extensive exploration and innovative solutions are pivotal in enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of peroxide detection methods, ultimately improving the quality assessment of edible oils for various industrial and consumer purposes.

In recent years, the incorporation of heteroaromatic dyes into biosensors has gained significant attention as a promising approach for detecting peroxides. There exists a growing demand for sensors capable of rapidly and efficiently measuring peroxide values in real-time and on-site. The convergence of distinctive characteristics inherent in heteroaromatic moieties—such as the ability to induce color changes, non-toxicity, long-lasting compounds, and high stability—alongside their reactivity toward peroxides, has established a formidable tool for peroxide detection across various analytical settings, particularly in applications related to food (Dickinson and Chang, 2008; Gülcin, 2012) and environmental monitoring (Kadam and Hong, 2022; Okeke et al., 2022).

Heterocyclic structures commonly found in these compounds, containing nitrogen or oxygen atoms, possess unique chemical properties that render them reactive towards peroxides (Escarpa, 2012). For instance, nitrogen atoms within heterocyclic aromatic systems like pyridine or imidazole rings can act as Lewis bases, enabling them to coordinate with peroxide molecules and partake in redox reactions. The detection of peroxides often relies on electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups within a single moiety. This phenomenon depends on the electronic effects induced by these groups, significantly influencing the compound's reactivity towards peroxides. For example, electron-donating groups (such as amino or hydroxyl groups) can enhance the molecule's nucleophilicity, increasing its reactivity towards peroxides. Conversely, electron-withdrawing groups (like nitro or carbonyl groups) can withdraw electron density, potentially impacting reaction kinetics or specificity.

The compounds outlined in Scheme 1 showcase an intricate array of heteroaromatic moieties designed to function as biosensors for detecting peroxides, both within cellular environments and in vitro settings. Scheme 1 provides a detailed illustration of these compounds, primarily divided into distinct categories such as acridine dyes, xanthene dyes, quinone-imine dyes, azo dyes, and nitro dyes, each serving specific roles in peroxide detection. Acridine dyes constitute a notable subset featuring compounds like acridine orange (1), acridine yellow (2), and proflavine (3), distinguished by their unique structures and functionalities. Meanwhile, xanthene dyes exhibit further classification into fluorenes (like acridine red (4), pyronine (5), pyronine Y (6), pyronine B (7), rhodamine B (8), rhodamine 123 (9), tetramethylrhodamine (10), kiton red S (11), rhodamine B benzyl ester (12), butyl rhodamine B (13), rhodamine 6 G (14), and rhodamine 800 (15)) and fluorones (comprising erythrosine sodium (16), fluorescein sodium (17), salicyl fluorone (18), tetrachlorotetraiodofluorescein (19), and tetrachlorofluorescein (20)), each demonstrating varying capacities for peroxide detection and other sensing mechanisms. The category of quinone-imine dyes encompasses diverse compounds, including azines like safranine T (21) and neutral red (22), oxazines such as nile blue (23), nile red (24), oxonine (25), oxazine 1 (26), Meldola’s blue (27), and brilliant cresyl blue (28). Also, thiazins like thionine (29), azure A (30), methylene blue (31), toluidine blue (32), and new methylene blue (33), each contributing unique properties and sensing capabilities toward peroxide detection and beyond. Furthermore, azo dyes, generally nonfluorescent, and nitro dyes stand out within this scheme, particularly due to their possession of the 2,3-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]oct-2-ene chromophore (DBO) structure (34), with DBO-Amine (35) being an exceptional case exhibiting fluorescence.

The compound classes exhibit diverse functionalities and structural variations, underlining their significant roles in various scientific domains such as photochemistry, analytical chemistry, and biochemistry (Dsouza et al., 2011). In environmental and life sciences applications, these fluorescent dyes bear particular relevance, notably in detecting peroxides within cellular or in vitro environments. However, the transformation of a fluorescent dye into a chemosensor or a chemosensing ensemble demands the incorporation of recognition motifs specifically tailored for detecting peroxides or related analytes.

Peroxides, particularly hydroperoxides, are unstable compounds generated in the initial stages of lipid oxidation. Their formation and breakdown are influenced by various factors, including oxygen levels, fatty acid composition, temperature, and the presence of metals. Increased oxygen partial pressure in the headspace intensifies dissolved oxygen in oil, consequently promoting oil oxidation (Choe and Min, 2006). The rate of peroxide production and accumulation depends on fatty acid composition and degree of unsaturation. These peroxides can undergo diverse decomposition pathways, yielding secondary products such as aldehydes, ketones, and other volatile compounds. These secondary products often contribute to the undesirable off-flavors, odors, and color changes associated with oxidized food items. Intermediate species, like alkoxy radicals and peroxy radicals, emerge during lipid oxidation, playing a pivotal role in propagating oxidative chain reactions. These radicals can abstract hydrogen atoms from neighboring lipid molecules, initiating a cascade of reactions. To regulate this process, introducing a reactive functional group from heteroaromatic dyes can be employed to terminate the chain reaction (Refer to Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unique interaction of heteroaromatic dyes with peroxides
ce:section.ce:para: When heteroaromatic moieties encounter peroxides like H2O2, a chemical reaction takes place, leading to alterations in the electronic structure of the dyes and resulting in changes in their optical properties. The reactivity of heteroaromatic dyes towards peroxides can originate from atoms bonded within the heteroaromatic moieties or from functional groups within these moieties. Scheme 2 illustrates the selected mechanisms involving acridine, fluorenes, rhodamine, and fluorones dyes with H2O2 in cellular environments.

For instance, structure (1), rooted in the acridine heterocyclic framework, serves as a fluorescent probe distinguishing between DNA and RNA and acts as a staining agent for cellular structures (Lyles and Cameron, 2002). Its photophysical characteristics exhibit significant pH dependence (Shaikh et al., 2008), with fluorescence emission peaking at 535 nm and a Stokes shift of approximately 40 nm in its protonated state (pKa around 9.5–10). Under neutral conditions, a shift towards longer wavelengths occurs, accompanied by an expanded Stokes shift of approximately 110 nm, although the fluorescence quantum yield remains low at Φf = 0.041 at pH 7 (Shaikh et al., 2008).

The catalytic activity of structure (4), observed in the iron from chloro iron(III) 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-pyridyl)porphyrinate, might be attributed to the typical acidic nature of iron porphyrins (Martins et al., 2023), which is intensified by the strong electron-withdrawing porphyrin ligand due to extensive fluorination. The iron-hydroperoxy-species [Fe(III)-OOH], formed by the coordination of H2O2 to the metal center and subsequent deprotonation, has been considered the active oxidant in the catalytic cycle (Rebelo et al., 2005).

In addition, the degradation of (8) within the H2O2/Hydroxyalamine or Cerium (IV)/H2O2/Hydroxylamine system showcased a declining removal rate over time (Wang et al., 2018). This reduction in removal rate is attributed to the high initial concentration of (8), which promotes efficient collisions between its molecules and radical peroxide (HO•). However, as the reaction proceeds, the concentration of (8) decreases while coproduct concentrations rise. This shift in concentrations heightens the collision rate between HO• and coproducts, shifting the interactions away from HO• with (8) molecule, ultimately leading to a decrease in the removal rate of (8) during the later stages of the reaction.

On the other hand, a distinct interaction of heteroaromatic dyes with peroxide is illustrated in Scheme 3, revealing a non-fluorescent probe that transforms into fluorescent dyes upon reacting with H2O2. The non-fluorescent probe (36) comprises three essential components: a chlorine atom, an azide group, and an active carbonyl, displaying unique nucleophilicity towards H2O2. This probe demonstrates exceptional selectivity for H2O2 and exhibits a linear increase in fluorescence intensity across a broad range of H2O2 concentrations, showing a substantial 127-fold enhancement in fluorescence intensity post-reaction with H2O2 (Zhuang et al., 2017). By employing a pseudo-first order model, the reported data yielded a reaction rate constant of 5.7 × 10−2 s−1 at 25°C. Although slightly smaller, this value compares well with previously documented fluorescence probes targeting H2O2 based on boronate chemistry (Dickinson et al., 2010) and Baeyer-Villiger type reactions (Abo et al., 2011).

Zhu et al. presented a study detailing the characteristics of the non-fluorescent probe (37), which functions as a mitochondria-targeted fluorescent probe (Zhu et al., 2016) specifically designed for highly reactive oxygen species (hROS), such as ClO– and HO•. This probe is constructed on a hybrid cyanine–phenothiazine platform. The non-fluorescent probe (37) showcased ratiometric absorption (A470 nm/A550 nm) and an "off/on" fluorescence response (I470 nm and I595 nm) upon exposure to ClO– and HO•. These responses were attributed to the oxidation of the sulfur atom within the phenothiazine moiety, resulting in the concurrent disruption of the π conjugation of the cyanine. Importantly, the non-fluorescent probe (37) enabled the ratiometric visualization of hROS generation specifically within cellular mitochondria.

The commercially available dye Amplex Red (N-Acetyl-3,7-dihydroxy-phenoxazine) (38) transforms from a non-fluorescent enzyme substrate to a highly fluorescent reaction product when exposed to H2O2 (Zhou et al., 1997). However, the determination of H2O2 utilizing Amplex Red necessitates the presence of peroxidases (POx), which presents challenges for precise quantitative analysis in complex biological samples or living cells due to potential enzymatic activity fluctuations in such environments. Nevertheless, (38) has garnered widespread use, notably in microwell plate-based assays that involve determining enzymes that either consume (such as peroxidases, catalases) or produce H2O2 (like glucose oxidase, cholesterol oxidase, choline oxidase). It provides a detection limit of 50 nM for H2O2 (Schäferling et al., 2011). The resulting oxidation product, resorufin 1, displays red fluorescence (λmax = 587 nm) and has an absorption maximum at 563 nm. Employing coupled enzymatic reactions, probe (38) assays with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) are utilized for detecting various analytes including glucose, cholesterol, acetylcholine, glutamic acid, and pyrophosphate

Larger structures, such as nano-graphene, carbon dots, and quantum dots, incorporate heteroaromatic moieties. Notably, the use of fluorescent nano-graphene or carbon dots for detecting Fe3+ ions has garnered considerable interest (Wang et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013). This detection mechanism relies on the specific interaction between the phenolic hydroxyl group and Fe3+ ions, crucial in various biological processes like red blood cell formation, oxygen transport, and DNA/RNA synthesis. Li et al. highlighted the potential of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (PL GQDs) as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection due to their abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups (Zhou et al., 2013). Their experiments involved analyzing emitted light from P-GQDs (λex = 400 nm) with varying Fe3+ concentrations, revealing decreased light intensity as Fe3+ concentrations increased. A notable trait of functionalized GQDs is their capability to absorb and emit visible light wavelengths. Particularly, P-GQDs emit excitation-independent photoluminescence at a peak wavelength of λem 550 nm when stimulated by visible light at λex 490 nm (Chen et al., 2013), representing the longest emission wavelength reported for GQDs. These distinctive properties position P-GQDs as robust tools for bio-imaging and bio-labeling, making them viable for detecting peroxide levels in food products.

Recently, Ma et al. made a discovery in identifying a unique type of interaction known as noncovalent surface-π stacking (Ma et al., 2021). This interaction occurs between heteroaromatic groups and peroxide-modified titania nanosheets, a connection not commonly associated with each other. This discovery significantly expands our understanding of noncovalent interactions and introduces a novel application of nanomaterials based on these interactions. Previously, it was believed that noncovalent π stacking occurred primarily between heteroaromatic molecules and planar structures like graphene. However, their research demonstrated that this interaction can also take place between aromatic groups and the specific nanosheets under investigation (Ma et al., 2021). This finding broadens the spectrum of materials capable of engaging in noncovalent π stacking interactions with peroxides found in food products. However, the use of these nanoparticles presents challenges in precisely determining the binding sites for peroxide detection in such cases.

The sensitivity and selectivity of heteroaromatic dyes in peroxide detection are determined by several critical factors. Firstly, the chemical structure of these dyes significantly influences their interaction with peroxides and subsequent signaling. For instance, the presence of electron-donating groups like amino (-NH2) or hydroxyl (-OH) on the aromatic ring of the dye, as seen in dyes such as Nile Red or Rhodamine B, can enhance sensitivity by promoting electron transfer processes with peroxides (Shah, 2014). Conversely, electron-withdrawing groups like nitro (-NO2) found in dyes such as Nitrotyrosine or Quinaldine Red may reduce sensitivity due to their electronic effects. Secondly, the functional groups attached to the aromatic ring, such as nitro, amino, or hydroxyl groups, significantly impact reactivity. Nitro groups present in dyes like Nitro Blue Tetrazolium or Nitrotyrosine can facilitate electron transfer, thereby enhancing sensitivity (Zahir et al., 2017). Additionally, hydroxyl groups found in dyes like Hydroxyquinoline or Hydroxynaphthol Blue might contribute to reactivity with peroxides. Moreover, pH and temperature conditions are crucial; maintaining an optimal pH range (e.g., pH 7–9 for many dyes) and temperature (e.g., around room temperature) ensures maximal signal intensity during detection (Qing et al., 2019). Finally, potential interfering substances like antioxidants (e.g., ascorbic acid), pigments (e.g., carotenoids), or metal ions (e.g., copper, iron) should be considered. For instance, ascorbic acid can interfere with the oxidation-reduction processes of certain dyes, leading to false-positive results (Sota-Uba et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2012). Properly identifying and accounting for such substances aids in enhancing the selectivity and accuracy of peroxide detection methods employing these dyes.

The use of heteroaromatic dyes for peroxide detection in food analysis offers several advantages, alongside significant limitations that require careful consideration. These attributes encompass essential factors such as stability, susceptibility to interference from other food components, detection limits, and the potential for false positives or negatives.

Heteroaromatic dyes offer distinct advantages in determination of peroxide detection. Firstly, their sensitivity and selectivity stand out as these dyes display inherent reactivity toward peroxides, leading to observable color changes or fluorescence signals, enabling sensitive and precise detection of peroxides (Ottone et al., 2021). Moreover, a highly fluorescent compound called 9-(4-(5-(quinolin-2-yl)-1 H-tetrazol-1-yl)phenyl)-9 H-carbazole demonstrates excellent sensitivity and selectivity, enabling the detection and quantification of hydrogen peroxide at tracer concentrations as low as 0.1 μM, with a quantification limit down to 2 μM (Halder et al., 2018). The cost-effective synthesis of the nanocatalyst is notable. This derivative exhibit strong analytical performance in detecting low levels of hydrogen peroxide, showcasing the success of this green synthesis approach. Secondly, these dyes provide rapid analysis compared to conventional methods such as iodometric titration. They yield swift colorimetric or fluorescent responses, expediting efficient and timely assessment of peroxide levels. For instance, the sensing system involves the catalytic oxidation reaction of glucose via glucose oxidase (GOx), generating H2O2. In the presence of Fe2+, the 580 nm emission of carbon dots under 365 nm excitation decreases due to H2O2, while the fluorescence emission at 450 nm of thiochrome increases due to the oxidation reaction between H2O2/Fe2+ and thiamine (Liu et al., 2020). This ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system on a smartphone platform is poised for sensitive and convenient determination of glucose and peroxide, particularly advantageous for biomedical analysis. Thirdly, the simplicity of the detection process using these dyes is notable; it's often straightforward, user-friendly, and requires minimal sample preparation and instrumentation. This user-friendliness enhances accessibility, allowing their use even in non-specialized laboratory settings. For instance, Zhu et al. developed a straightforward and user-friendly probe based on 7-diethylamino-coumarin, a heteroaromatic dye, to detect biologically relevant primary diamines like cadaverine and putrescine (Zhu et al., 2021). The reaction resulted in a blue emission band at 475 nm. This probe facilitated both colorimetric and fluorescence detection of primary diamines, demonstrating remarkable selectivity and a low limit of detection (209 nM). Additionally, Ye et al. introduced a multifunctional fluorescent heteroaromatic dye based on julolidine-fused coumarin for detecting freshness in meat (Ye et al., 2022). These probes underwent an aza-Michael addition followed by an elimination reaction with cadaverine to generate a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence response.

Heteroaromatic dyes, while offering several advantages, present notable disadvantages in peroxide detection within food samples. Stability concerns arise as these dyes might degrade or react with other food components during storage or analysis, potentially leading to inaccurate results and undermining the reliability of peroxide determination, as evidenced in studies by Dalkiran and Brett, 2021 and Mohammadi and Jafari, 2020. Moreover, certain food components like pigments, antioxidants, and emulsifiers can interfere with peroxide detection using heteroaromatic dye precursors, resulting in false readings or hindering accurate assessment (Enaru et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2020). Additionally, while heteroaromatic dyes demonstrate high sensitivity, there are detection limits beyond which accurate quantification becomes challenging. This limitation may restrict their application to samples with higher peroxide levels or require additional sample dilution or concentration steps, as illustrated in works by Hakami et al., 2020. Furthermore, these dyes might exhibit cross-reactivity or responses to compounds other than peroxides, leading to potential false positives or negatives. This issue compromises the specificity of the analysis and necessitates careful interpretation of results (Bezner et al., 2019; Kua et al., 2022).

Ongoing research is focused on overcoming these limitations by improving the efficiency of heteroaromatic dyes in peroxide detection analysis. Strategies involving the refinement of precursor design, addressing matrix interferences, and optimizing calibration/validation protocols are actively being explored to enhance stability, selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy. Therefore, while the utilization of heteroaromatic dyes in peroxide detection analysis presents advantages such as sensitivity, rapid analysis, and simplicity, challenges related to stability, interference from food components, detection limits, and the potential for erroneous results necessitate careful consideration. Diligent efforts in addressing these challenges through continuous research and development can fully unleash the potential of heteroaromatic dyes as reliable tools for peroxide detection analysis.

In the domain of peroxide detection in food, the potential of heteroaromatic dyes holds significant promise for advancements, especially in improving sensitivity, selectivity, and real-time monitoring capabilities. One avenue involves innovating novel heteroaromatic dyes designed for more effective reactions with peroxides. Researchers aim to enhance sensitivity and selectivity by modifying the structures and functional groups of these dyes. Equally crucial is integrating these precursors into detection platforms, leveraging innovative techniques like nanomaterials, microfluidic devices, and biosensors to develop more efficient detection systems. Moreover, there is a need to explore strategies for quantitatively analyzing the results associated with these dyes, refining calibration and validation protocols to ensure precise peroxide determination across diverse food matrices.

Concurrently, there is an increasing emphasis on developing multi-analyte detection systems capable of assessing various indicators of oxidative deterioration in food products. Integrating heteroaromatic dyes with other sensing elements has shown promise in achieving effective multi-analyte detection. Additionally, expanding the applications of heteroaromatic dyes beyond peroxide detection presents an attractive prospect. Exploring other aspects of food analysis, such as secondary oxidation products and antioxidant activity, could significantly broaden the applicability of these dyes in assessing overall food quality.

In conclusion, future research in the realm of heteroaromatic dyes for peroxide detection should prioritize augmenting sensitivity, selectivity, and real-time monitoring capabilities. This advancement involves refining precursor design, optimizing detection platforms, improving quantitative analysis techniques, exploring multi-analyte detection strategies, adopting emerging technologies, and venturing into new application domains. These collective strides will elevate methods for evaluating oxidative deterioration in food products, ensuring enhanced food quality, safety, and consumer satisfaction.

Nurdiana Nordin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hairul Anuar Tajuddin: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Arif Zuhair Mohd Arnuar: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Zanariah Abdullah: Writing – review & editing.
